subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose ECG criteria for sodium bicarbonate
stem:
You are the emergency department registrar managing a 28-year-old woman brought in by ambulance 90 minutes after a witnessed ingestion of around 2 grams of dothiepin tablets with vodka. She is drowsy but rousable to voice, agitated when stimulated. Her partner reports she took the tablets after an argument and a long history of depression. Initial GCS 13, BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 128, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 95% room air.

The ECG just performed shows sinus tachycardia at 128 bpm, QRS duration 142 ms with a prominent R wave in aVR of 5 mm and a deep S wave in lead I.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58 mmHg, HR 128, RR 22, SpO2 95%, T 36.6 °C, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, agitated on stimulation, dry mucous membranes, dilated pupils 6 mm reactive, no focal neurology, no seizure activity
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia 128, QRS 142 ms, R in aVR 5 mm; VBG pH 7.32, pCO2 41, HCO3 21; Na 138, K 3.6; paracetamol level pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube and obtain repeat ECG and serum drug levels in 4 hours before any additional pharmacological intervention beyond standard supportive measures
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms with hypotension and an R wave in aVR over 3 mm meets criteria for immediate sodium bicarbonate; activated charcoal alone is inadequate given the cardiac toxicity. (Source: Murray Toxicology Handbook 3rd ed)

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol (100 mL of 8.4%) IV bolus, repeat to keep QRS under 100 ms and serum pH 7.45 to 7.55, intubate for airway protection given GCS 13 and start IV crystalloid 1 L bolus
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms, R in aVR over 3 mm, hypotension and CNS depression in TCA overdose are indications for sodium bicarbonate boluses titrated to QRS narrowing and pH 7.45-7.55, plus intubation for airway and crystalloid resuscitation. (Source: Murray Toxicology Handbook 3rd ed)

options.text: Commence IV metoprolol 5 mg slow bolus to control the tachycardia and reduce myocardial oxygen demand, repeat ECG in 30 minutes and titrate further beta-blockade based on heart rate response
options.rationale: Beta-blockade in TCA overdose worsens hypotension; tachycardia is compensatory for vasodilation and direct cardiac toxicity. (Source: Murray Toxicology Handbook 3rd ed)

options.text: Administer flumazenil 0.2 mg IV titrated to GCS over 15 and avoid sodium bicarbonate unless clearly seizing because rapid alkalinisation may precipitate cardiac arrhythmia in TCA toxicity
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed or unknown overdoses (precipitates seizures); rapid alkalinisation is the indicated treatment, not a hazard. (Source: Murray Toxicology Handbook 3rd ed)

options.text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes to suppress polymorphic ventricular ectopy and reserve sodium bicarbonate for QRS over 160 ms or established ventricular tachycardia
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is indicated at QRS over 100 ms, not over 160 ms; magnesium alone does not address sodium channel blockade. (Source: Murray Toxicology Handbook 3rd ed)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose presents with anticholinergic features, CNS depression, cardiovascular toxicity (sodium channel blockade) and seizures. ECG markers of significant toxicity include QRS over 100 ms (sensitivity ~80%), R wave in aVR over 3 mm, terminal R/S ratio in aVR over 0.7, and rightward axis. Sodium bicarbonate boluses (1 to 2 mmol/kg, often 100 mmol in adults) are indicated for QRS over 100 ms, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia or seizures. Target pH 7.45 to 7.55. Intubation and hyperventilation aid alkalinisation. IV lipid emulsion and ECMO are last-line. Activated charcoal only if airway secured and within 1 to 2 hours of ingestion.
key_points: TCA toxicity ECG: QRS over 100 ms, R in aVR over 3 mm
Sodium bicarbonate: 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, target pH 7.45-7.55
Intubate for GCS under 9 or for hyperventilation to aid alkalinisation
Avoid: flumazenil, beta-blockers, class Ia/Ic antiarrhythmics, physostigmine
Refractory: lipid emulsion, ECMO support
explanation.pearls: Apparent benzodiazepine-responsive seizures in TCA overdose still trigger urgent sodium bicarbonate because the underlying mechanism is sodium channel blockade; once a TCA seizes the patient is at imminent risk of cardiac arrest from the same channel block.

sources.label: Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists Toxicology Network

subtopic_detail: Lorazepam challenge for diagnosis and treatment of catatonia
stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to the medical ward to see a 22-year-old woman with a recent first episode of psychotic depression on the psychiatric unit who was transferred to medicine 36 hours ago for poor oral intake. She has eaten almost nothing and drunk very little for 5 days. The medical team are concerned she may have an organic encephalopathy.

On examination she shows marked psychomotor retardation, will not respond to verbal commands, has waxy flexibility with maintenance of posed positions for over a minute, intermittent grimacing, mutism with occasional brief echolalia and posturing of the right arm. T 37.1 °C, BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 92. Bloods show no infection or metabolic cause; CT head is unremarkable. CK is 290 U/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.1 °C, BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 98%, GCS reported as 14
data_table.Examination: Waxy flexibility, mutism, posturing, grimacing, echolalia, Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale 18
data_table.Investigations: Bloods unremarkable, CK 290, CT head unremarkable, EEG pending, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg orally daily and review for response after 72 hours given that catatonia in psychotic depression usually resolves with effective antipsychotic monotherapy at adequate doses
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and may precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; benzodiazepines (lorazepam challenge) are first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Initiate enteral feeding via nasogastric tube to address nutritional compromise from 5 days of minimal intake, monitor electrolytes daily for refeeding syndrome and delay specific catatonia treatment until oral intake is restored
options.rationale: Nutritional support is appropriate but should not delay catatonia-specific treatment; the catatonia itself is reversible with lorazepam in most cases and treating it will restore oral intake. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for empirical immunotherapy with high-dose methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily for 5 days and consider plasma exchange given the possibility of NMDA receptor encephalitis presenting with catatonia and mutism
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis is a consideration but lorazepam challenge is the recommended next step and gives both diagnostic and therapeutic information rapidly. NMDAR antibodies, EEG and LP are part of workup. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV (lorazepam challenge), observe over 10 to 15 minutes for partial or complete remission of catatonia (around 50% rate), then commence scheduled lorazepam 2 mg three times daily and refer for ECT if no response within 48 to 72 hours
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge is the standard initial step for suspected catatonia; partial improvement supports the diagnosis and informs ongoing scheduled lorazepam. ECT is indicated if no response in 48-72 hours or for malignant catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue current management without specific pharmacotherapy and arrange daily Bush-Francis Rating Scale assessment, planning ECT only if catatonia persists beyond 14 days and not responding to supportive care
options.rationale: Withholding pharmacotherapy delays both diagnosis and treatment; lorazepam challenge is recommended on the day of presentation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a treatable syndrome with motor, behavioural and autonomic features. Recognition relies on the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale or DSM criteria (at least 3 of mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, echolalia, echopraxia, mannerisms, stereotypy, grimacing, agitation, mutism). The lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV or sublingual) achieves partial or complete response in about 50% within 10 minutes; this both confirms the diagnosis and is the start of treatment. Scheduled lorazepam 2 mg three times daily is continued. ECT is highly effective and is recommended if there is no response in 48-72 hours, for malignant catatonia (autonomic instability, hyperthermia), or for life-threatening features. Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolves due to NMS risk.
key_points: Diagnosis: 3+ of mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, echolalia (DSM)
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV challenge: 50% partial response within 10 minutes
Scheduled lorazepam 2 mg TDS as ongoing first-line
ECT if no response in 48-72 hours or for malignant catatonia
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolves (NMS risk)
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (hyperthermia, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis) clinically overlaps with NMS and serotonin syndrome; in either picture, withhold antipsychotics, give benzodiazepines, and consider urgent ECT if there is no rapid improvement.

sources.label: Sienaert et al. A clinical review of catatonia

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD diagnosis confirmation pathway and driving notification under Austroads
stem:
You are the GP registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman who self-presented requesting assessment for adult ADHD after seeing content online. She describes lifelong inattention, distractibility and impulsivity that she now believes contributed to her difficulties at school and her current poor work performance at her professional services firm. She reports six or more inattentive symptoms (loses focus mid-task, forgets appointments, struggles to start tasks) and four hyperactive-impulsive symptoms with onset in primary school.

Her teachers' reports document poor concentration from age 8. She has no current substance use, no current depression or anxiety meeting criteria, and no medical comorbidities. She holds an unrestricted Australian car licence and drives daily to work.

data_table.Examination: Normal physical exam, no cardiovascular abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Hospital ECG normal; thyroid function normal; iron studies normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence a trial of methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg twice daily and review response at 4 weeks to assess symptom improvement before referring to a psychiatrist for formal diagnosis and ongoing prescribing
options.rationale: GPs cannot initiate stimulants in adult ADHD in most Australian states; an authority psychiatrist (or in some states paediatrician) must confirm the diagnosis and initiate stimulant therapy under Schedule 8 authority. (Source: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA))
source_refs: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

options.text: Reassure her that adult-onset ADHD is uncommon and that her symptoms are more likely to reflect anxiety or burnout; provide CBT referral and reassess in 6 months without specific ADHD pathway investigation
options.rationale: She describes longstanding childhood-onset symptoms documented by teachers, not adult-onset, and meets symptom thresholds; CBT alone without considering ADHD misses the diagnosis. (Source: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA))
source_refs: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily titrated to 80 mg as a non-stimulant ADHD pharmacotherapy that does not require psychiatrist authority and can be initiated in GP practice for clear adult ADHD presentations
options.rationale: Atomoxetine still requires diagnostic confirmation before initiation; AADPA recommends formal diagnosis through a specialist with structured assessment, not GP commencement on screening features. (Source: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA))
source_refs: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

options.text: Order EEG, MRI brain and overnight polysomnography in primary care to exclude differential diagnoses including absence seizures, structural intracranial lesion and obstructive sleep apnoea before any referral for ADHD assessment
options.rationale: Routine EEG, MRI and polysomnography are not indicated unless specific clinical features suggest these diagnoses; this delays appropriate ADHD pathway. (Source: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA))
source_refs: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

options.text: Refer her to a psychiatrist for diagnostic confirmation by structured interview, screen with a validated adult ADHD tool (DIVA-5 or ASRS-v1.1), assess comorbidity, and counsel on the Austroads requirement to disclose untreated symptomatic ADHD if it impairs safe driving
options.rationale: AADPA recommends specialist diagnostic confirmation with validated tools (DIVA-5, ASRS), comorbidity assessment, and driver-licensing implications under Austroads should be discussed where symptoms could affect driving. (Source: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA))
source_refs: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD requires symptoms onset before age 12 (DSM-5), persistence into adulthood, functional impairment in two or more settings, and not better explained by another condition. The AADPA guideline (2022) is the current Australian standard. Diagnosis is by specialist (psychiatrist) using structured interview such as DIVA-5 plus screening tools (ASRS-v1.1). Stimulant initiation requires Schedule 8 authority; ongoing prescribing can be shared-care with GPs in some states. Atomoxetine, guanfacine and bupropion are non-stimulant options. Austroads requires disclosure when symptoms (with or without treatment) might affect safe driving; most well-controlled adults can hold an unconditional licence.
key_points: DSM-5 ADHD: childhood onset (under 12), persistent, impairing, 2+ settings
Specialist diagnosis with DIVA-5 or ASRS-v1.1
Stimulants are Schedule 8: authority psychiatrist (or paeds) initiates
Non-stimulants: atomoxetine, guanfacine, bupropion
Austroads: disclose if symptoms affect driving; conditional licence possible
explanation.pearls: Adult ADHD presentations in women are commonly missed because hyperactivity manifests as internal restlessness and inattention dominates; longstanding sense of underperformance, chronic disorganisation and burnout out of proportion to load is a soft red flag to screen formally rather than label as anxiety.

sources.label: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD (AADPA)

subtopic_detail: Varenicline versus NRT versus bupropion choice in post-myocardial infarction smoker
stem:
You are the GP registrar reviewing a 56-year-old man 8 weeks after an inferior NSTEMI treated with primary PCI to the right coronary artery. He smokes 25 cigarettes daily and has been on aspirin, ticagrelor, atorvastatin, ramipril and metoprolol since discharge. He started smoking at 16 and has had 3 prior cessation attempts using cold turkey alone, each relapsing within 3 weeks.

His mood is stable and he has no history of seizures, depression or self-harm. He has no renal impairment. He scores 8 on the Fagerstrom test for nicotine dependence (high). He wants to quit and asks what works best for him given his recent heart attack.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 64, BMI 28, SpO2 98%, expired CO 28 ppm
data_table.Examination: Healthy male, no cardiac signs of decompensation, no neurological abnormality
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs normal; recent echocardiogram LVEF 50%; HbA1c 5.8%

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation?

options.text: Prescribe nicotine inhalator alone titrated to cravings, suggest he sets a quit date 2 weeks from now and provide brief advice plus a self-help quit booklet for behavioural support over the next 12 weeks
options.rationale: Single-form NRT is less effective than combination NRT or varenicline; given high dependence (Fagerstrom 8) a more effective regimen should be offered. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation guide)

options.text: Defer pharmacotherapy until 6 months post-MI because both varenicline and bupropion have been associated with neuropsychiatric and cardiovascular adverse events in the immediate post-infarct period and only NRT is appropriate now
options.rationale: Varenicline is safe and effective in stable post-MI patients beyond 2 weeks; deferral until 6 months loses a key teachable moment. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation guide)

options.text: Commence varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily from day 8 for 12 weeks with quit date at day 8 to 14 and Quitline behavioural support throughout therapy
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest evidence for sustained 6-month abstinence; the EAGLES trial confirmed cardiac and neuropsychiatric safety. Standard 12-week course with quit date at day 8-14 plus Quitline. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation guide)

options.text: Prescribe bupropion 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 12 weeks with quit date at day 8 to 14 and Quitline support, choosing bupropion because it has the best efficacy and safety profile in post-MI patients
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but slightly less so than varenicline; it is contraindicated in seizure disorder, eating disorders and severe hepatic impairment. Varenicline has stronger evidence post-MI. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation guide)

options.text: Prescribe combination NRT (21 mg/24h patch plus 4 mg gum as needed) with no behavioural support given his prior failed attempts and ask him to return only if cessation fails at 4 weeks for escalation of therapy
options.rationale: Combination NRT is a reasonable second choice but is less effective than varenicline and behavioural support (Quitline) significantly increases abstinence; not offering it is suboptimal. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation guide)

explanation.summary: Combined behavioural support (Quitline) plus pharmacotherapy is the most effective approach. Varenicline (TGA-approved, 12-week course, quit date at day 8-14) has the highest 6-month abstinence rates per EAGLES and Cochrane network meta-analyses. Combination NRT (patch plus oral) is similar in efficacy. Bupropion is effective but contraindicated in seizure disorder, eating disorders and severe hepatic impairment. EAGLES confirmed varenicline and bupropion neuropsychiatric and cardiovascular safety in patients with and without mental health conditions. Post-MI is a high-leverage teachable moment; cessation halves 5-year mortality. Pregnant women are typically offered NRT (oral forms preferred), and adolescents NRT only.
key_points: First-line: varenicline OR combination NRT (patch + oral)
Bupropion: contraindicated in seizures, EDs, severe hepatic disease
EAGLES (2016): varenicline cardiovascular and neuropsychiatric safety confirmed
Behavioural support: Quitline 13 78 48 adds substantially to pharmacotherapy
Pregnancy: NRT (oral preferred); adolescents NRT only
explanation.pearls: Schedule the quit date 8 to 14 days after starting varenicline so the patient is steroid-stated on the medication; ramping the dose first reduces nausea, which is the main reason for early discontinuation.

sources.label: EAGLES trial: Anthenelli et al. Lancet 2016

subtopic_detail: Acute severe behavioural disturbance pharmacological choice in young adult male
stem: You are the emergency department registrar called to the resuscitation bay where a 25-year-old man with first-presentation psychosis is acutely agitated, threatening staff and other patients, attempting to leave despite an Inpatient Treatment Order. De-escalation by an experienced nurse and security has failed over 15 minutes. He is not currently taking any psychotropic medication and has no documented allergies. Collateral suggests no significant alcohol or substance use in the past 24 hours but possible methamphetamine use 3 days ago. He weighs around 80 kg and is haemodynamically stable. The duty psychiatrist has authorised chemical restraint.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/88 mmHg, HR 118, T 37.0 °C, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, ECG sinus tachycardia QTc 430 ms
data_table.Examination: Pacing, fists clenched, verbally threatening, no signs of intoxication, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.2 mmol/L, urine drug screen pending, FBC and UEC pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single first-dose chemical restraint?

options.text: Administer droperidol 10 mg intramuscularly, monitor continuous cardiac monitoring and SpO2, repeat 10 mg IM at 15 minutes if not settled, and review for further treatment after 30 minutes from the second dose
options.rationale: NSW Health rapid tranquillisation guidance for acute severe behavioural disturbance in adults endorses droperidol 10 mg IM as a first-line option, with repeat at 15 minutes and standard monitoring. (Source: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 25 mg IM together as a combined sedation regimen, monitor for extrapyramidal effects and lower the threshold for ECG monitoring given the QT interval
options.rationale: Haloperidol-promethazine combination is no longer first-line in Australian ED rapid tranquillisation; droperidol IM (or olanzapine IM, midazolam IM in some protocols) is preferred. (Source: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments

options.text: Administer midazolam 10 mg IM as a single agent without monitoring beyond intermittent vitals given that benzodiazepine monotherapy avoids the cardiac and extrapyramidal risks of antipsychotic chemical restraint
options.rationale: Midazolam IM monotherapy is an option in protocols but mandates continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring (respiratory depression risk); intermittent vitals are inadequate. (Source: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments

options.text: Administer ketamine 4 mg/kg intramuscularly under anaesthetic supervision as the first-line agent for severe behavioural disturbance in a stimulant-related agitation given recent methamphetamine use
options.rationale: Ketamine is a tertiary option (failed other agents) and is given by experienced clinicians with airway capability; not first-line in standard ED protocols. (Source: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments

options.text: Offer oral olanzapine wafer 20 mg with a glass of water and observe for cooperation before escalating to any parenteral medication, regardless of the failed de-escalation by experienced staff already attempted
options.rationale: Oral medication assumes cooperation that has clearly already failed; parenteral chemical restraint is required for documented refractory severe behavioural disturbance. (Source: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments

explanation.summary: Australian ED protocols (NSW Health, Victorian DHHS) for acute severe behavioural disturbance prioritise de-escalation first. If failed, first-line parenteral options include droperidol 10 mg IM (repeat at 15 min, max 20 mg), olanzapine IM 10 mg or midazolam IM 5 to 10 mg, with continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring. Avoid combinations of benzodiazepines and antipsychotics where possible due to additive respiratory depression. Ketamine 4 to 5 mg/kg IM is a third-line option with airway-capable clinician present. Document indication, dose, monitoring and review interval. Apply restraint principles consistent with the local Mental Health Act.
key_points: De-escalation first (15 min); chemical restraint if failed
First-line IM: droperidol 10 mg (repeat at 15 min) OR olanzapine 10 mg OR midazolam 5-10 mg
Continuous SpO2 and cardiac monitoring required post-dose
Avoid routine combination of benzodiazepine + antipsychotic
Ketamine 4-5 mg/kg IM is tertiary option with airway support
explanation.pearls: Document the failure of de-escalation, the specific indication, the consent or legal authority (e.g. Mental Health Act ITO), the dose, route and time, and the planned review at 15 minutes; the audit trail matters as much as the choice of agent in subsequent medico-legal review.

sources.label: NSW Health: Management of patients with acute severe behavioural disturbance in emergency departments
